Richard F. Thomas (ed.), Virgil: Georgics. Vol. 2, Books III- IV. Cambridge 1988.
Foster argues that passages in the plague narrative (G. 3, 470-477;
509-514; 531-536) are meant to recall both Caesar (cf. В. G. 6, 28) and
Octavian and their subjugation of northern tribes. Segal surveys the literary
treatment of the Orpheus myth as the myth of the essence of poetry, its ability
to encounter the fullest possible intensity of beauty and sorrow and to transform
them into song. Vergil and Ovid claim the lion's share in ancient treatments.
Segal treats the continuity of the myth in H. D. (Hilda Doolittle), Rukeyser,
Rich, Ashberry, and Rainer Maria Rilke.

1989.
Harrison notes that Vergil extends the bounds of military epic by usin
epic material and by treating his war as a civil struggle with no easy jin
answers. Roman and heroic traditions emerge in the depiction of d
fighting. Vergil is a true Roman in his glorification of the state and it
men. Thaniel's "sea approach to the art of Vergil" treats drowned pe
the epic with sidelong glances at Greek poetic parallels. Wiltshire's stud
that Vergil developed strategies for reconciling the public and private:
ters act in the public realm while maintaining self-distance, offer hospit
strangers, perform sustained hard work {labor), and develop a new pietas

raw_text:
Robert McKay Wilhelm, "Cybele: The Great Mother of the Augustan
Order," Vergilius 34 (1988) 77-101.
The late John Ferguson detects assimilated Epicureanism in the Bucolics
along with a rejection of specific Epicurean doctrines. The pax Augusta made
quietistic philosophy irrelevant and Vergil's move away from Epicureanism is
reflected in both the Georgics and the Aeneid. However, some residual
Epucureanism remains in his characterization of Aeneas. Henry's searching
study of time remembered, prophecy, and the religious element in the epic is
sensible and suggestive. Perkell's review of the theodicy brings new thoughts on
a time-honored dilemma for the poet's audience and some fresh approaches to
the Golden Age in Vergil's version. Wilhelm's illustrated article accents the role
of Cybele in the epic and in Augustan cult service. The Ara Pacis relief of the
"madonna-like" seated figure may include traits of Cybele in its composite
nature.

despicable slayer of Volscian Camilla, no doubt reflects the hostile tradition.

Ahl's study of soundplay and wordplay finds intriguing alignment with Ferdinand de Saussure's study of the role of hypograms and anagrams as points of
departure for literary composition in Vergilian and other contexts (#241).
Roman Jakobson & Lawrence C. Jones, Shakespeare's Verbal Art (Mouton
1970) offer intriguing parallels in English verse. Donaldson suggests that the
adjective dirae in epic always carries the same emotional charge as the proper
noun, Dirae. Duelos examines nine similes in A. 7-12 in which Aeneas and
Turnus are brought together in an 'almost autonomous world' of imagery, a natural world of violence and destruction ultimately mastered by the individual
who will found the Roman nation. De Neubourg reviews J. Hellegouarc'h's
Hexamètre et métrique verbale (Paris 1985).

Paolo Venini, "Suli' imitazione virgiliana in Valerio Fiacco,"
Athenaeum 67 (1989) 273-275.
Brooke treats Vergilian elements used to express Christian Veritas. Paulinus
of Nola evokes Vergil's epic as a basis for critical barbs at Aeneas, the gods and
pagan morality. So aemulatio of the pagan literary tradition blends indissolubly
with religious polemic. De Franciscis traces Aeneid 2, 1 in Pompeian graffiti.
Reineke shows that Scaliger referred to Macrobius only as a rough model for
his own comparisons. Spence's terse and lucid analysis accents the reception
and transformation of Ciceronian rhetoric in Vergil's epic, Augustine's Confessions, Christian doctrine, and the lyrics of early troubadours.

humanist theme. Erasmus called him "a Christian Virgil." Fleming notes

Troilus and Criseyde. Flusser exhumes a Hebrew short story, with accompanying
